paper_title: A statistical study of Weinmannia pollen trajectories across the Andes

methods.description: The trajectory of airborne pollen was simulated using the HYSPLIT model to understand the movement and concentration of pollen across regions.

steps.description: The Final Run (FNL)-Southern Hemisphere (SH) Archive furnished by NOAA ARL was used as meteorological input to HYSPLIT.
steps.searchable_summary: fetch meteorological data from NOAA ARL FNL-SH Archive

steps.description: Backward trajectories starting at the San Carlos de Bariloche aerobiological station were calculated for specific days when Weinmannia pollen was detected.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate backward trajectories using HYSPLIT model

steps.description: Running time lasted from 24 to 30 hours for each trajectory simulation.
steps.searchable_summary: set simulation running time for trajectory calculations

steps.description: Correspondence between trajectories and the position of potential sources were checked through GIS maps.
steps.searchable_summary: validate trajectory data using GIS maps

methods.description: T-Mode PCA with Varimax rotation was used to obtain leading spatial anomaly patterns of geopotential height associated with simulated trajectories.

steps.description: Daily NCEP-NCAR reanalyses were used, with a 2.5° by 2.5° horizontal resolution.
Three vertical levels (1000, 850, and 700 hPa) were selected to represent the lower troposphere.
Daily anomaly fields of geopotential heights were selected for dates when Weinmannia pollen was detected.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze daily anomaly fields for specific dates

steps.description: PC Scores and loadings were calculated to show primary types of anomalies and their correlation with observed fields.
1000 hPa fields corresponding to the 2 cases with the highest correlation to PC patterns were selected for detailed analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: select high-correlation cases for detailed analysis
